title: Bug: no-op --state done on a gated issue records spurious gated->gated event
description:
## Problem

Re-running `jit issue update <id> --state done` on an issue that is already in the `gated` state (because it has pending/failed gates) records a spurious `gated -> gated` entry in `events.jsonl` and bumps the issue's `updated_at` timestamp, even though no real state transition occurred.

This corrupts the audit log: any consumer reading `events.jsonl` or `updated_at` (metrics, velocity, stalled-work detection, validation analysis) will treat a no-op retry as genuine progress.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Take an issue with a pending gate (e.g. `code-review`).
2. Run `jit issue update <id> --state done`. It transitions `ready -> gated` (correct) and reports the blocking gate.
3. Run `jit issue update <id> --state done` again (a retry).
4. Inspect `events.jsonl`: a second `issue_state_changed` event with `{"from":"gated","to":"gated"}` is appended, and `updated_at` advances.

## Expected Behavior

When an issue is already `gated` and remains blocked by the same gates, the retry should be a no-op for the event log: do not append an `issue_state_changed` event and do not bump `updated_at`. The blocking-gate message may still be shown.

## Root Cause

`handle_gate_blocking` in `crates/jit/src/commands/issue.rs:659-683` unconditionally emits `new_issue_state_changed(..., old_state, State::Gated)` even when `old_state` is already `Gated`, producing the synthetic `gated -> gated` event.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Retrying `--state done` on an already-`gated` issue (still gate-blocked) appends no `issue_state_changed` event to `events.jsonl`.
- [ ] `updated_at` is not advanced by such a no-op retry.
- [ ] The blocking-gate guidance is still surfaced to the user on the retry.
- [ ] A regression test covers retrying `--state done` on an already-gated issue and asserts no new state-change event is emitted.
- [ ] A genuine `ready -> gated` (or any real) transition still emits its event as before.

## Extended Scope (broadened 2026-06-05 during review)

Code review surfaced that the spurious-progress signal is not unique to the `--state done` retry: `jit issue update` also routes `--add-gate`, `--remove-gate`, `--assignee`, and `--unassign` through helpers that saved unconditionally, and `update_issue`'s final save was unconditional. Any of these as a semantic no-op (re-adding an existing gate, removing an absent gate, re-assigning the same assignee, unassigning an already-unassigned issue, or an `issue update` whose flag values match the current values) still bumped `updated_at`. Scope expanded to make all of these idempotent.

### Additional Success Criteria

- [ ] Re-adding a gate that already exists does not bump `updated_at`.
- [ ] Removing a gate that is not present does not bump `updated_at`.
- [ ] Re-assigning the same assignee does not bump `updated_at`.
- [ ] Unassigning an already-unassigned issue does not bump `updated_at`.
- [ ] `issue update` with field flags whose values equal the current values (idempotent edit) does not bump `updated_at`, while a real edit still persists.
- [ ] Regression tests cover each idempotent no-op path.
